caveats: a: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than six-digit 326121.
a: Task shares are inferred from occupation groups and workflow knowledge, not directly measured.
a: DOE documents feasible manufacturing applications, not realized labor substitution.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business product sales, commonly quoted per unit, weight, or production run under purchase orders and supply agreements; customer-specific tooling and qualification can support recurring revenue.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive profile-extrusion departments, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, resin producers, and firms whose main activity belongs to plastic film, sheet, pipe, or laminated products.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of unlaminated plastic rods, tubes, and other profile shapes that repeatedly supply external OEM, distributor, and construction customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for all NAICS 326100 plastics-product manufacturers, not 326121 or task exposure. I map the relatively small management, business, computer, and office shares plus portions of inspection, machine tending, scheduling, maintenance, and material handling to AI-addressable tasks, and weight toward the larger production payroll. The broader-industry mix may overstate molding work and understate extrusion work; treating only task fractions as substitutable biases the result downward versus occupation-level automation claims.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit NAICS 326100 rather than six-digit 326121.
Task shares are inferred from occupation groups and workflow knowledge, not directly measured.
DOE documents feasible manufacturing applications, not realized labor substitution.
a.change_evidence: A 326121 plant-level time-and-wage study separating quoting, scheduling, quality inspection, extrusion tending, changeovers, maintenance, packing, and material movement, plus current adoption data for machine vision and closed-loop extrusion control.
a.rationale: The 2023 BLS table shows 605,750 workers in plastics-product manufacturing, with 25.97% in metal/plastic-working occupations, 11.29% miscellaneous assemblers, and substantial hand material-handling and packaging employment, while management is 5.09% and business/financial work 3.51%. AI can assist quoting, production planning, documentation, vision inspection, predictive maintenance, and exception handling, but much extrusion, changeover, packing, and maintenance work remains physical.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all plastics-product manufacturing (NAICS 326100) from $190.7 billion in 2024 to $212.4 billion in 2034, a 1.1% compound annual increase. Applying that growth for five years gives about 1.056, used as the center for 326121; profile shapes may be more construction- and industrial-cycle-sensitive than the broad category, so the range is widened in both directions.
caveats: BLS projection is four-digit and ten-year, not six-digit and five-year.
The Plastics Industry Association figures cover the wider plastics value chain.
Construction-linked profile demand can deviate materially from broad plastics output.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year volume forecasts and shipment histories specifically for U.S. plastic profiles, segmented by windows/doors, construction trim, transportation, electrical, and industrial uses, including substitution and recycled-content assumptions.
d5.rationale: Plastic profile demand should broadly track construction, transportation, equipment, and fabricated-product activity, with substitution toward lightweight and corrosion-resistant materials offset by cyclical building exposure, resin-price volatility, environmental pressure, and competing materials. The broad BLS real-output projection supports roughly flat-to-modest growth rather than structural disappearance.

caveats: Eligibility is not directly observed and the frozen firm count is margin-bridged.
Contract manufacturers with concentrated customer books may technically qualify but be unattractive or fragile transfer targets.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm classification of the 128 frozen LMM-band candidates using customer concentration, captive status, repeat-order share, primary NAICS activity, and normalized EBITDA quality.
e.rationale: Most independent profile extruders sell repeat physical output to outside customers and therefore fit the recurring outsourced-supply lens. Ineligibility is concentrated in captive operations, firms dominated by one-off tooling or unrelated fabrication, distressed businesses lacking transferable customer relationships, and records whose true primary activity falls in adjacent plastics codes.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 326121 ($1,769,714,988) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 326121 ($9,174,458,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $487,847 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 326121); class <5: 53 firms, avg revenue/firm $906,396 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $132,787 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,878,659 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $275,223 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 35 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,032,743 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $737,297 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,438,931 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,529,303 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $50,557,439 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,406,665 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $178,551,697 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,157,824 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 128

caveats: No direct study measures operator requirement for 326121.
Automation could reduce headcount substantially without removing the need for a legally and operationally accountable manufacturer.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and make-versus-buy data covering planned insourcing, additive manufacturing, imports, and replacement of extruded profiles by metal, wood composite, or other systems.
o.rationale: The current basket is physical, specification-bound product requiring accountable manufacture, material control, extrusion, inspection, and delivery. Software can reduce labor and improve process control but cannot self-serve the physical profile. Residual displacement risk comes from customer insourcing, additive or alternative manufacturing, standardized imports, and material substitution.

caveats: Contract structure and customer power are not observed.
The estimate concerns retention of implemented gross benefit, not implementation success or demand volume.
q.change_evidence: A sample of customer contracts and quote histories showing price-adjustment clauses, annual cost-down obligations, resin-index mechanisms, rebid cadence, gross-margin bridges, and retention of documented automation savings.
q.rationale: Per-unit and supply-agreement pricing lets an operator retain a meaningful portion of productivity gains before renewal. Retention is limited by competitive rebids, open-book or indexed resin pass-through, customer productivity demands, and the visibility of cycle-time and scrap improvements to sophisticated OEM buyers. Customer-specific tooling and qualification can slow repricing but do not prevent it.

caveats: No source measures five-year implementation specifically for profile extrusion.
Implementation will vary sharply by production volume, SKU complexity, and installed controls.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of 326121 plants reporting current MES/ERP and sensor coverage, AI pilot-to-production conversion, measured labor hours saved, false-reject rates, and payback periods by use case.
rho.rationale: Within five years, established tools for computer-vision quality control, predictive maintenance, quoting, document processing, and production scheduling can be deployed, but fragmented plant data, integration with legacy extruders, small-firm capex limits, customer qualification, false-reject risk, and required human accountability constrain conversion of exposed tasks into avoided labor.

caveats: No six-digit deal-flow or owner-age series was located.
Aging ownership can lead to internal succession or closure rather than a qualifying external control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and proprietary broker transaction counts for U.S. plastics profile extruders over 2016-2025, matched to the eligible-firm denominator, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability in the low twenties is consistent with ordinary private-company ownership turnover plus succession-driven exits, reduced for family/internal succession, indefinite holds, minority recapitalizations, failed sale processes, and plants that close rather than transfer as going concerns.

principal_driver: AI-enabled quality, planning, maintenance, and administrative workflows layered onto repeat physical production.
principal_weakness: Six-digit evidence is scarce, and the dominant production work is physical while OEM pricing pressure can share savings away.
